blocks.content: The problem — the specific shape of the guilt she turned over in herself now, staring at the ceiling of her childhood room — was not that she hadn't told Ethan the truth. She wasn't sure she owed him the truth yet. They were new. The thing she was carrying was hers to share on her own terms and her own timeline and she had never believed otherwise.
The problem was subtler than that.

Every day she didn't tell him, he got further in. Every conversation, every skee-ball tournament, every evening at the Italian place with the candlelight — he was building something in himself, something real and personal, something that was going to matter to him. And he was building it with incomplete materials. Not because she was lying. But because some truths, when they finally arrive, change the weight distribution of everything that came before them.

She had watched it happen with other people. The moment of knowing. The way a face reorganizes itself around new information. The specific silence of someone recalibrating — how long it takes, what it means, what they decide to do next.

She had been through that silence with people she was less invested in than Ethan. She had learned to read it, manage it, survive it. But with him it would be different. With him she cared what happened on the other side of it.
She pressed her palm flat against her chest. Not dramatically — she wasn't the type for dramatic gestures alone in the dark. Just to feel the steadiness of it. To confirm it was there.
